one_line_takeaway: Box-free generative-model watermarks are removable by exploiting the verification decoder's gradients; the paper's Decoder Gradient Shields (a closed-form output shield plus two orthogonal-perturbation variants) reorient/rescale those gradients to block remover training, achieving 100% defense success rate with sub-millisecond overhead for the output variant.
key_finding: Without any defense the removal attack works: R's loss is driven to 10^-7 to 10^-12 and the decoder outputs a nearly blank watermark while keeping high image quality (average PSNR 37.95 dB deraining / 29.61 dB image-gen, MS-SSIM 0.9968 / 0.9806). With any DGS variant deployed, R's loss stalls around 10^-2 and never meaningfully decreases; for DGS-O the true loss ||Z - W0|| actually trends upward and plateaus above 1.2, so the remover cannot learn. The headline result is a defense success rate (SR) of 100% under every tested setting, including mismatched attacker loss functions (L1, or L2+consistency loss instead of the assumed L2), all four watermark images, gradient sign flipping (for DGS-O when eigenvalues Λi are in [10^-6,10^-5] or smaller), the P=I Z-restoration attempt, and heavy post-processing (JPEG down to 10% quality, WGN at 0 dB, lattice attack removing 1 in 3 pixels). DGS-O is by far the cheapest (0.083 ms deraining / 0.133 ms T2I) versus DGS-I/DGS-L in the 0.03-0.07 s range.
core_contribution: The paper makes two coupled security contributions to box-free DNN watermarking (where a copyright watermark is embedded into a generative model's high-entropy image outputs and read back by a dedicated decoder D). First, it identifies and formalizes a decoder-side vulnerability that prior work overlooked: because the decoder D is jointly trained with the encoder E and exposed via a black-box verification API, an attacker can obtain gradients backpropagated through D and use them to train an image-to-image 'watermark remover' network R that strips the watermark before building a surrogate model. Second, as the defense (the paper's actual novel artifact), it proposes Decoder Gradient Shields (DGSs), a family of three provable mechanisms that perturb the decoder's gradient to prevent R from converging while preserving watermark-extraction fidelity: DGS-O (a closed-form reorientation/rescaling of the decoder OUTPUT via a positive-definite matrix P, Z* = -P*Z + (P+I)W), DGS-I (an orthogonality-guided adversarial perturbation on the decoder INPUT, FGSM-inspired but chosen orthogonal to the removal-loss gradient so legitimate outputs are unchanged), and DGS-L (the same orthogonal perturbation applied at an intermediate LAYER for lower cost and hidden location). The box-free watermarking scheme being protected (Zhang et al. [17],[18]) is cited prior work used as the victim model, NOT a contribution of this paper. This is an extended journal version of the authors' CVPR 2025 paper [1], which covered only DGS-O; DGS-I, DGS-L, RGB experiments, and the T2I task are new here.
threat_model: Model owner deploys box-free watermarking (private M and E, plus a black-box API for the watermark decoder D used for ownership verification). Defender is either a limited-trust security provider with full access to D but no right to modify M (Scenario 1) or the model owner itself with full internal access (Scenario 2). Attacker wants an untraceable surrogate of the protected model M: it queries M as a normal user to collect watermarked outputs Y, then trains a remover R to minimize a removal loss L_Removal = ||D[R(Y)] - W0||^2 (driving the decoded watermark toward the null watermark W0) plus a fidelity loss ||R(Y)-Y||^2 to keep image quality. Deliberately attacker-friendly assumptions: the attacker can query D freely to check for the watermark AND can directly obtain the exact gradient of D w.r.t. its input (no estimation error), knows all non-decoder gradient components, and may detect the defense and try to counter it (gradient sign flipping, assuming P=I to restore Z, or alternative removal losses). Out of scope: attacks that bypass D entirely by training a separate surrogate to approximate the extraction function [46].
limitations: The authors themselves flag: (1) DGS-O's closed-form nature is a theoretical weakness -- if both W and P were known an attacker could invert Z = -P^-1 Z* + (I+P^-1)W; they show the practical P=I approximation fails, but call fully robust Z-restoration an open question, which is why DGS-I/DGS-L exist. (2) DGS-L needs access to intermediate-layer outputs, so it only works in Scenario 2 (defender = model owner). (3) The whole DGS family only defends gradient-based removal that routes through the decoder; attacks that bypass D by training a separate surrogate to approximate extraction [46] are explicitly out of scope and left for future work. Reviewer-noted caveats: the evaluation assumes an unusually strong-for-the-defender, weak-in-one-sense attacker (exact gradients, no estimation noise), the perfect 100% SR across all conditions leaves no margin to compare variants quantitatively (differences are shown mainly via loss curves and runtime), and all experiments use a single victim watermarking framework (Zhang et al. [18]) and one CNN/UNet architecture, so generalization to other box-free schemes is argued rather than measured.

datasets_used: PASCAL VOC [40] for the image-deraining task: 12,000 images split into two 6,000-image subsets (victim model vs. remover); rainy inputs synthesized via Zhang & Patel [41]
Text-to-image (T2I) generation: 12,000 text prompts split evenly (victim vs. remover), images generated with Stable Diffusion [42]
Four watermark images for sensitivity tests: text image (W1), binary pattern (W2), complex image (W3), solid color (W4)
quantitative_results: Defense success rate (SR) = 100% (1.00) under ALL tested settings
No-defense removal attack drives R's loss to 10^-7 to 10^-12; with DGS, loss stalls around 10^-2
DGS-O true loss ||Z - W0|| trends upward and converges above 1.2 (attack fails)
No-defense removed-image fidelity: PSNR 37.9523 dB (deraining), 29.6088 dB (image gen); MS-SSIM 0.9968 (deraining), 0.9806 (image gen)
Robustness to JPEG (Table II) down to 10% quality: SR=1.00 (PSNR 26.8063 deraining / 26.8010 gen at 10%)
Robustness to additive white Gaussian noise (Table III) at 0 dB: SR=1.00 (PSNR ~1.72 dB, MS-SSIM ~0.305)
Robustness to lattice attack [32] (Table IV) at step 2 (1 in 3 pixels perturbed): SR=1.00 (PSNR ~12.27, MS-SSIM ~0.64)
Runtime (Table V): DGS-O 0.083 ms deraining / 0.133 ms T2I; DGS-I 0.071 s / 0.067 s; DGS-L-S 0.060 / 0.058 s; DGS-L-M 0.047 / 0.046 s; DGS-L-D 0.035 / 0.033 s
Computational complexity: DGS-O O(d^2), DGS-I O(l*d^2), DGS-L O((l-k)*d^2) for l-layer CNN, d x d RGB output
Training: remover R is a UNet; 100 epochs, Adam, lr 0.0002, loss weights alpha1=alpha2=beta1=beta2=1; hardware single NVIDIA RTX 5000 Ada 32 GB GDDR6
DGS-O robust to gradient sign flipping when eigenvalues Λi in [10^-6,10^-5] or smaller
baselines_compared: Victim / protected watermarking scheme: Zhang et al. state-of-the-art box-free model watermarking [18] (extension of [17]) -- the system being defended, not a competitor
Attack baseline: the paper's own gradient-based removal attack with no defense (loss reaches 10^-7..10^-12), used as the reference the DGSs must defeat
Attacker loss-function variants compared: L2 (assumed/matched), L1, and L2+consistency loss [45]

Prior gradient-manipulation defenses discussed as related work (not for generative watermarking): prediction poisoning [36] and gradient redirection [35], described as recursive, costly, and ineffective against hard-label extraction [37]
Post-processing / adaptive attacks evaluated against DGS-O: gradient sign flipping, Z-restoration (P=I assumption), JPEG compression, WGN, and the lattice attack of Liu et al. [32]
